stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 28-year-old G2P1 RhD-negative woman at 32 weeks gestation who presents 6 hours after a moderate motor vehicle accident with seatbelt restraint. She has mild lower abdominal tenderness but no per vaginal bleeding. The CTG is reassuring and ultrasound shows no retroplacental haematoma. Her booking blood group is O Rhesus negative with no antibodies on routine screening at 28 weeks (after standard antenatal anti-D). The fetus is known to be RhD positive on cell-free DNA testing.

data_table.Examination: Soft uterus with mild tenderness; no per vaginal bleeding; CTG reassuring; abdomen non-peritonitic
data_table.Investigations: Group O Rh negative; antibodies negative on 28-week screen; Kleihauer test result 3.5 mL fetal blood; ultrasound: no retroplacental haematoma, EFW 1900 g
data_table.Medications: Routine antenatal anti-D given at 28 weeks (625 IU)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Give an additional 625 IU intramuscular anti-D within 72 hours and assume the routine 28-week dose covers any further sensitising event during this admission
options.rationale: The Kleihauer indicates fetomaternal haemorrhage exceeding the volume covered by 625 IU; dose must be calculated to cover the actual volume of fetal red cells detected. (Source: NBA Guidelines on Anti-D)

options.text: Calculate additional anti-D dose based on Kleihauer (125 IU per 1 mL fetal red cells) and administer within 72 hours; recheck Kleihauer in 48 hours
options.rationale: Sensitising events in RhD-negative women carrying RhD-positive fetus require Kleihauer-guided additional anti-D (125 IU per 1 mL fetal RBC) on top of any routine prophylaxis, given within 72 hours and rechecked if large bleed suspected. (Source: NBA Guidelines on Anti-D)

options.text: Withhold further anti-D because routine 28-week dose has been given and the Kleihauer simply reflects normal background fetal cell transfer
options.rationale: Sensitising events (trauma) require an additional dose regardless of routine antenatal anti-D; Kleihauer volumes above 6 mL fetal red cells require additional anti-D dosing. (Source: NBA Guidelines on Anti-D)

options.text: Give 1500 IU intramuscular anti-D once as a fixed dose without Kleihauer-based calculation and arrange routine follow up only
options.rationale: Fixed-dose 1500 IU covers up to 12 mL fetal red cells; with 3.5 mL already documented and ongoing trauma a Kleihauer-guided calculated dose is recommended to prevent underdosing. (Source: NBA Guidelines on Anti-D)

options.text: Defer anti-D pending repeat antibody screen at 36 weeks; administer only if anti-D antibodies are detected at that time
options.rationale: Anti-D prophylaxis after a sensitising event must be given within 72 hours; waiting for antibody development means sensitisation has already occurred and anti-D no longer prevents it. (Source: NBA Guidelines on Anti-D)

explanation.summary: Sensitising events in RhD-negative women with an RhD-positive fetus include trauma, antepartum haemorrhage, external cephalic version, amniocentesis and chorionic villus sampling. Anti-D 625 IU IM covers up to 6 mL of fetal red cells; Kleihauer testing quantifies fetomaternal haemorrhage and additional anti-D at 125 IU per mL of fetal red cells is given when volume exceeds the routine dose coverage. Anti-D must be given within 72 hours of the sensitising event. Routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 weeks does not eliminate the need for additional event-based dosing.
key_points: Sensitising events need event-specific anti-D within 72 hours
Kleihauer quantifies fetomaternal haemorrhage volume
Standard 625 IU covers 6 mL fetal red cells; add 125 IU per mL above
Cell-free DNA fetal RhD typing avoids unnecessary anti-D
explanation.pearls: Cell-free DNA fetal RhD genotyping from 11 weeks allows targeted antenatal anti-D only when fetus is RhD positive, avoiding unnecessary blood-product exposure in 40 percent of RhD-negative women carrying RhD-negative fetus.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar in the birth suite called urgently to a multiparous woman at 38 weeks who has just had spontaneous rupture of membranes during artificial rupture for slow progress. She is 4 cm dilated. The midwife reports feeling a pulsating loop of cord at the introitus. The CTG shows a sustained fetal bradycardia of 80 bpm. The woman is not contracting at this moment. The on-call anaesthetist and theatre team have been called. The operating theatre is 5 minutes away.

data_table.Examination: Pulsating cord palpable at the introitus; cervix 4 cm dilated; head at -2 station; CTG bradycardia 80 bpm sustained
data_table.Investigations: Bedside clinical assessment; no time for further imaging

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Wait for the cervix to reach full dilatation then perform instrumental vaginal delivery once the head is engaged at the spines
options.rationale: At 4 cm dilatation and station -2 the head is not deliverable instrumentally; waiting for full dilatation risks irreversible fetal compromise. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord prolapse)

options.text: Replace the cord above the presenting part through the cervix; complete labour vaginally if bradycardia resolves within 5 minutes
options.rationale: Manual cord replacement is not recommended due to vasospasm risk and high recurrence; immediate category 1 caesarean with manual presenting-part elevation is standard. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord prolapse)

options.text: Augment labour with oxytocin to expedite vaginal delivery while monitoring the fetal heart with continuous CTG and frequent review
options.rationale: Oxytocin increases cord compression and worsens fetal compromise; cord prolapse mandates urgent operative delivery rather than augmentation. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord prolapse)

options.text: Manually elevate the presenting part with a vaginal hand; position knee-chest or left lateral; activate category 1 caesarean
options.rationale: Confirmed cord prolapse with sustained fetal bradycardia is a category 1 caesarean indication; manual cord protection by elevating the presenting part off the cord and maternal positioning are required en route to theatre. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord prolapse)

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 micrograms subcutaneously and reassess fetal heart rate in 10 minutes before deciding on delivery mode
options.rationale: Tocolysis is adjunctive when delay to theatre is anticipated but does not replace immediate manual cord elevation and category 1 caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord prolapse)

explanation.summary: Umbilical cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency with category 1 caesarean indication when delivery is not imminent vaginally. Immediate management: call for help, declare category 1, manually elevate the presenting part with a vaginal hand to relieve cord compression, position knee-chest or left lateral or Trendelenburg, keep cord in vagina if visible (do not handle excessively to avoid vasospasm), consider bladder filling with 500 to 700 mL via catheter to lift presenting part, tocolysis (terbutaline 250 micrograms SC) if delay is anticipated. Delivery within 30 minutes from diagnosis is the standard.
key_points: Category 1 caesarean: 30-minute decision-to-delivery target
Manual elevation of presenting part throughout transfer
Knee-chest or Trendelenburg positioning
Tocolysis as adjunct when delay anticipated
explanation.pearls: Risk factors for cord prolapse include high presenting part, polyhydramnios, multiple pregnancy, malpresentation, prematurity and artificial rupture of membranes with unengaged head; controlled ARM at vaginal examination reduces risk.

stem: You are the gynaecology registrar reviewing a 58-year-old postmenopausal woman taking tamoxifen 20 mg daily for 4 years following ER-positive breast cancer. She presents with 3 weeks of intermittent painless vaginal spotting. She has no other medications, no obesity (BMI 24), no family history of Lynch syndrome, and no diabetes. Pelvic ultrasound shows an endometrial thickness of 10 mm with heterogeneous appearance. Pipelle biopsy demonstrates atypical endometrial hyperplasia (endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia).

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft; bimanual: anteverted uterus, non-tender, no adnexal mass; speculum: small amount of fresh blood at cervix
data_table.Investigations: TVUS endometrium 10 mm heterogeneous; Pipelle: atypical hyperplasia (EIN); FBC, UEC, LFT unremarkable; CA-125 22
data_table.Medications: Tamoxifen 20 mg daily; calcium and vitamin D

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Insert a levonorgestrel intrauterine system and repeat endometrial sampling at 6 months as first-line conservative therapy
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel-IUS is the conservative option only in patients without atypia or where fertility preservation is required and surgery is contraindicated; in atypical hyperplasia postmenopausal, hysterectomy is preferred. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometrial hyperplasia)

options.text: Refer to gynaecological oncology for total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy after multidisciplinary discussion
options.rationale: Atypical endometrial hyperplasia (EIN) in a postmenopausal woman carries up to 40 percent risk of concurrent endometrial cancer; total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is the standard of care after MDT review. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometrial hyperplasia)

options.text: Switch tamoxifen to letrozole and repeat endometrial biopsy in 3 months without surgical intervention
options.rationale: Switching to an aromatase inhibitor may be appropriate after consultation with oncology but does not address the established atypical hyperplasia and concurrent cancer risk; surgery is needed regardless. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometrial hyperplasia)

options.text: Provide oral medroxyprogesterone 20 mg daily for 3 months and reassess endometrial thickness on ultrasound
options.rationale: Oral progestogen is conservative therapy for non-atypical hyperplasia in pre-menopausal women or fertility-sparing settings; postmenopausal atypia warrants definitive surgery. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometrial hyperplasia)

options.text: Repeat the Pipelle biopsy in 3 months to confirm persistence before any surgical referral or specialist consultation
options.rationale: Atypical hyperplasia confirmed on biopsy is a surgical indication; repeating Pipelle delays definitive management and risks missing concurrent carcinoma. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometrial hyperplasia)

explanation.summary: Endometrial hyperplasia is classified as hyperplasia without atypia or atypical hyperplasia (endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia, EIN). Tamoxifen increases endometrial cancer risk 2 to 3 fold. Postmenopausal bleeding requires endometrial assessment by transvaginal ultrasound (endometrial thickness greater than 4 mm warrants sampling) and Pipelle biopsy. Atypical hyperplasia has up to 40 percent concurrent endometrial cancer rate at hysterectomy and is treated by total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy in postmenopausal women. Hyperplasia without atypia is treated with progestogens (oral or LNG-IUS) with surveillance biopsies.
key_points: Atypia in postmenopausal endometrium: hysterectomy + BSO
Up to 40 percent concurrent endometrial cancer risk at hysterectomy
Without atypia: progestogen, LNG-IUS preferred
Tamoxifen doubles to triples endometrial cancer risk
explanation.pearls: All postmenopausal bleeding warrants assessment regardless of how trivial the volume; endometrial cancer is the most common gynaecological malignancy in Australia.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 26-year-old primiparous woman at 34 weeks. She presents with frontal headache, epigastric pain and visual disturbance. Her booking BP was 110/70. Her booking renal function and uric acid were normal. Today she is hyperreflexic with brisk reflexes and 3+ clonus. Antenatal aspirin 100 mg nocte was started at 14 weeks for prior unexplained pregnancy loss. CTG is reassuring. She has not yet had antenatal corticosteroids.

data_table.Examination: Hyperreflexic with 3+ clonus; mild facial oedema; epigastric tenderness; CTG reassuring
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 92, ALT 168, creatinine 96, uric acid 0.42, urine PCR 320 mg/mmol; lactate normal; coags normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Commence oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily and arrange induction of labour within 24 hours regardless of cervical favourability
options.rationale: Oral antihypertensive is too slow for severe-range BP with severe features; IV antihypertensive plus magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis is required immediately. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM only; defer antihypertensive and magnesium until corticosteroid course is complete in 24 hours
options.rationale: Antihypertensive treatment and magnesium seizure prophylaxis cannot wait for the steroid course; they are given concurrently while corticosteroid is administered. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Discharge with home BP monitoring and increased aspirin to 150 mg daily; daily community midwife review for fetal movements only
options.rationale: Severe-range BP with severe features (headache, visual disturbance, epigastric pain, deranged labs) is a medical emergency requiring inpatient management not outpatient care. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Proceed directly to category 1 caesarean section without antihypertensive or magnesium prophylaxis to avoid maternal seizure risk
options.rationale: Even when delivery is needed, antihypertensive control and magnesium prophylaxis must be initiated before transfer to theatre to reduce maternal stroke and eclampsia risk. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer IV labetalol or hydralazine to lower BP, IV magnesium sulfate 4 g load then infusion, and betamethasone for fetal lung maturation; plan delivery
options.rationale: Pre-eclampsia with severe features requires immediate IV antihypertensive to a target BP under 160/110, magnesium for seizure prophylaxis, betamethasone for lung maturity at 34 weeks if delivery within 7 days is anticipated, and plan for timely delivery. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Pre-eclampsia with severe features includes BP 160/110 or higher, severe headache, visual disturbance, epigastric pain, pulmonary oedema, oliguria, platelets under 100, ALT twice normal, creatinine over 90 or fetal growth restriction. Management is parallel: IV antihypertensive (labetalol 20 mg IV repeated to 80 mg, or hydralazine 5 to 10 mg IV) to target BP under 160/110; IV magnesium sulfate 4 g loading over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour infusion for at least 24 hours for seizure prophylaxis. Betamethasone is given if under 35 weeks and delivery is anticipated within 7 days. Delivery planning at the earliest gestation balances maternal and fetal risk and is usually 34 to 37 weeks for severe features.
key_points: Severe features mandate IV antihypertensive plus magnesium
Magnesium 4 g load then 1 g/hour for 24 hours minimum
Betamethasone if under 35 weeks and delivery anticipated
Delivery timing balances maternal severity and gestation
explanation.pearls: Magnesium toxicity presents with absent reflexes (over 4 mmol/L), respiratory depression (over 5 mmol/L) and cardiac arrest (over 6 mmol/L); treat with calcium gluconate 1 g IV.

stem: You are the gynaecology registrar reviewing a 32-year-old woman with 4 years of cyclical dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia, and dyschezia worsening over the past 6 months. She has tried ibuprofen 400 mg as needed and a combined oral contraceptive in standard 21/7 regimen with partial benefit. She does not desire pregnancy in the next 2 years and contraception is acceptable. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a 3 cm left ovarian endometrioma. Examination demonstrates uterosacral nodularity. She has had no surgery.

data_table.Examination: Tender posterior fornix; palpable uterosacral nodules; bimanual: fixed retroverted uterus; left adnexal fullness
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: 3 cm left ovarian endometrioma, kissing ovaries; CA-125 68; pregnancy test negative
data_table.Medications: Combined oral contraceptive 30 mcg ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel cyclic; ibuprofen PRN

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Switch to continuous combined oral contraceptive (no pill-free interval) plus regular paracetamol and reassess pain at 3 months
options.rationale: Cyclical pain on cyclic COCP often improves with continuous regimen to suppress menstruation; this is the next step before escalating to second-line hormonal or surgical management. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Refer immediately for laparoscopic excision of endometriosis and cystectomy of the left ovarian endometrioma without further medical trial
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failure of medical therapy, large endometriomas (greater than 4 cm) or fertility concerns; continuous COCP should be trialled first. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Commence GnRH analogue (goserelin 3.6 mg monthly) plus tibolone add-back therapy for 6 months as first-line escalation
options.rationale: GnRH analogues are typically second-line after continuous hormonal contraception or progestogen failure due to bone-density and vasomotor side effects requiring add-back therapy. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Recommend acupuncture and pelvic floor physiotherapy alone for 6 months without changing hormonal therapy
options.rationale: Adjuncts may help but in moderate to severe endometriosis with imaging-confirmed disease, escalation of hormonal suppression should not be deferred for 6 months. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Stop the combined oral contraceptive and commence the levonorgestrel intrauterine system as monotherapy for endometriosis symptoms
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is effective for endometriosis but switching from one hormonal contraception to another without first trialling continuous regimen is not the standard sequence; continuous COCP is simpler and equally efficacious. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

explanation.summary: Endometriosis management follows a stepwise medical-then-surgical approach guided by symptoms, imaging, fertility goals and previous treatments. First-line: NSAIDs plus combined hormonal contraception in continuous regimen, or progestogen (oral, depot or LNG-IUS). Second-line: GnRH analogues with add-back therapy. Surgery (laparoscopic excision or ablation) is for failed medical therapy, large endometriomas (greater than 4 cm), suspected deep infiltrating disease, or fertility concerns. Multidisciplinary care including pelvic floor physiotherapy and psychology improves outcomes.
key_points: Continuous combined hormonal contraception before escalation
GnRH analogue with add-back is second-line medical
Surgery for endometrioma over 4 cm or failed medical therapy
Pelvic floor physiotherapy and psychology as adjuncts
explanation.pearls: GnRH antagonists (elagolix, relugolix) are oral alternatives to depot GnRH agonists and avoid the initial flare phase but still require bone monitoring or add-back.

stem: You are the GP referring a 34-year-old woman who has had 3 consecutive first-trimester miscarriages, the most recent 8 weeks ago. She has no living children. Her menstrual cycle is regular. Her BMI is 23. She does not smoke. She and her partner are not consanguineous. There is no family history of thrombosis. She is preparing to conceive again and asks what investigations are warranted before the next attempt. She is currently on no medications and her TFTs done last year were normal.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable; no thyroid mass; no signs of androgen excess
data_table.Investigations: Previous miscarriages at 8, 10 and 9 weeks; no products sent for cytogenetics; baseline TFT normal a year ago
data_table.Medications: Pregnancy multivitamin and folate 500 mcg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate workup?

options.text: Antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotype, thyroid function tests, and pelvic ultrasound for uterine anomalies
options.rationale: Australian guidance after 3 consecutive losses recommends parental karyotype, antiphospholipid antibody screen, thyroid function and pelvic anatomy assessment as first-line workup, all with evidence of treatment options if abnormal. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Inherited thrombophilia screen (factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A, protein C and S, antithrombin) as the first-line workup
options.rationale: Routine inherited thrombophilia testing for recurrent first-trimester miscarriage is not recommended; the association is weak and no treatment is proven to improve outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Endometrial biopsy for chronic endometritis and uterine natural killer cell quantification as the primary investigation workup
options.rationale: Endometrial biopsy for NK cells and chronic endometritis is investigational; not recommended as primary workup in Australian or international guidance. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Routine hysteroscopy and pelvic MRI for all couples after a second miscarriage without prior ultrasound or biochemical assessment
options.rationale: Pelvic ultrasound is the first-line anatomical assessment; hysteroscopy and MRI follow only if ultrasound is abnormal or suggests a mullerian anomaly. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Sperm DNA fragmentation testing and male hormone profile as the first-line investigation in unexplained recurrent miscarriage
options.rationale: Sperm DNA fragmentation testing has no proven role in routine recurrent miscarriage workup; first-line investigations target maternal antiphospholipid, parental karyotype, thyroid and uterine anatomy. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

explanation.summary: Recurrent miscarriage is defined in Australia as 3 or more consecutive losses before 20 weeks (2 or more in some international guidelines). Causes include antiphospholipid syndrome (15 percent, treatable with aspirin plus heparin), parental balanced translocations (3 to 5 percent), uterine anomalies (10 to 15 percent), thyroid disease and unexplained (50 percent). First-line investigations: antiphospholipid antibodies (repeated 12 weeks apart for diagnosis), parental karyotype, thyroid function, pelvic ultrasound. Inherited thrombophilia screening is not routinely recommended because association is weak and treatment unproven.
key_points: Workup after 3 consecutive losses in Australia
Antiphospholipid syndrome is treatable cause (aspirin plus heparin)
Parental karyotype identifies balanced translocations
Inherited thrombophilia screen NOT routinely recommended
explanation.pearls: Antiphospholipid syndrome requires both clinical criteria (3 or more first-trimester losses, fetal death over 10 weeks, or severe early pre-eclampsia) AND persistent positive antibody on 2 tests at least 12 weeks apart.

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar admitting a 28-year-old man with schizophrenia who has failed adequate trials of risperidone and olanzapine at therapeutic doses over the last 18 months. He has ongoing positive symptoms and severe functional impairment. He has been counselled regarding clozapine therapy and has consented. He has no cardiac history, no fever, no neutropenia, and pre-clozapine baseline bloods including FBC, ECG, lipids and HbA1c are normal. He is registered on the clozapine patient monitoring system.

data_table.Examination: Settled, no extrapyramidal features; no abnormal cardiac findings
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 6.8, neutrophils 4.2; ECG: normal sinus, QTc 410 ms; troponin pending baseline; HbA1c 5.2; lipids normal
data_table.Medications: Olanzapine 20 mg daily (to taper); lorazepam 1 mg PRN

lead_in: What is the most appropriate clozapine initiation regimen?

options.text: Start at 200 mg twice daily and continue full dose without titration; monitor only WCC and neutrophils for safety
options.rationale: Starting at therapeutic dose causes severe orthostasis, sedation, seizures and arrhythmia; gradual titration is mandatory. (Source: AMH: Clozapine)

options.text: Defer clozapine and switch to aripiprazole 30 mg as a fourth antipsychotic trial; reserve clozapine for failure of aripiprazole
options.rationale: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia is defined as failure of 2 adequate antipsychotic trials; further antipsychotic delay clozapine, which is the only agent with proven superior efficacy in this group. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

options.text: Start at 100 mg twice daily and continue without dose titration unless side effects occur; monthly FBC monitoring is sufficient
options.rationale: Starting at 100 mg twice daily exceeds safe initiation rates and risks orthostasis, sedation and seizure; monthly FBC misses the high-risk neutropenia window in weeks 6 to 18. (Source: AMH: Clozapine)

options.text: Start at 25 mg twice daily with monthly FBC monitoring throughout the first 18 weeks regardless of dose changes
options.rationale: Starting dose is too rapid and monthly FBC misses the high-risk neutropenia period; weekly FBC for 18 weeks is the Australian standard. (Source: AMH: Clozapine)

options.text: Start at 12.5 mg in the evening on day 1; titrate by 25 to 50 mg per day to a target of 300 to 450 mg by 3 to 4 weeks with weekly FBC
options.rationale: Australian clozapine titration begins at 12.5 mg with gradual upward titration to therapeutic dose over 2 to 4 weeks; weekly FBC monitoring is mandatory for 18 weeks then monthly, with troponin and CRP weekly for first 4 weeks for myocarditis surveillance. (Source: AMH: Clozapine)

explanation.summary: Clozapine is the only antipsychotic with proven superiority in treatment-resistant schizophrenia. Initiation requires gradual titration starting at 12.5 mg evening day 1, then 25 mg day 2, increasing by 25 to 50 mg per day to a target of 300 to 450 mg by 3 to 4 weeks. Weekly FBC monitoring for the first 18 weeks then monthly captures clozapine-induced neutropenia (peak risk weeks 6 to 18). Weekly troponin and CRP for the first 4 weeks screens for myocarditis. Patients must be on a clozapine patient monitoring system. Seizure threshold lowered: avoid drugs and triggers.
key_points: Initiation: 12.5 mg day 1, titrate by 25 to 50 mg per day
Weekly FBC for 18 weeks, monthly thereafter
Weekly troponin and CRP first 4 weeks for myocarditis
Target 300 to 450 mg per day by 3 to 4 weeks
explanation.pearls: Smoking cessation can double clozapine plasma levels via CYP1A2 induction loss; warn patients and adjust dose monitoring if smoking status changes.

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 42-year-old man with schizophrenia who has been stable on olanzapine 15 mg nocte for 18 months. His last metabolic review was 9 months ago. He has gained 8 kg over the past year and reports increased thirst and polyuria. His mother has type 2 diabetes. He smokes 10 cigarettes daily and walks for 20 minutes most days. He drinks 2 standard drinks twice weekly. His latest set of bloods is back.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/88, HR 84, RR 16, SpO2 99% RA, T 36.6; BMI 33 (up from 29)
data_table.Examination: Central adiposity; no acanthosis nigricans; no thyroid mass
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 7.4 percent, fasting glucose 8.6 mmol/L; total cholesterol 5.8, LDL 3.6, HDL 0.9, triglycerides 3.2; ALT 48; eGFR 92; TSH 1.8

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Continue current antipsychotic and lifestyle changes alone; defer diabetes pharmacotherapy until HbA1c above 8 percent at next review
options.rationale: HbA1c 7.4 with symptomatic hyperglycaemia warrants pharmacotherapy initiation per Australian diabetes guidance; deferral risks complications. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Stop olanzapine abruptly and substitute haloperidol 5 mg daily as a lower-metabolic-risk first-generation antipsychotic option
options.rationale: Abrupt switch risks relapse and discontinuation syndrome; first-generation antipsychotics carry higher extrapyramidal burden and are not the standard substitute. (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring)

options.text: Continue olanzapine unchanged and add metformin 500 mg twice daily for newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes; lifestyle measures and statin discussion
options.rationale: Olanzapine has the highest metabolic risk among antipsychotics; switching is one option but in a patient stable for 18 months on a single agent, initiating metformin while continuing the antipsychotic is reasonable per Australian psychiatric metabolic guidance, with antipsychotic switch as a parallel consideration. (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring)

options.text: Switch olanzapine to clozapine to address treatment resistance and metabolic syndrome together as a single intervention
options.rationale: Clozapine has higher metabolic risk than olanzapine and is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia; switching solely for metabolic reasons worsens not improves the metabolic phenotype. (Source: AMH: Clozapine)

options.text: Cease olanzapine and offer cognitive behavioural therapy for schizophrenia as monotherapy until metabolic parameters normalise
options.rationale: Stopping antipsychotic in stable schizophrenia carries a 70 percent 1-year relapse rate; CBT is adjunctive not substitutive. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic-induced metabolic syndrome is most pronounced with olanzapine and clozapine. RANZCP monitoring schedule: weight, waist, BP, fasting lipids and glucose, HbA1c at baseline, 3 months, 6 months and annually. New onset metabolic disease (type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, hypertension) is managed in parallel with antipsychotic optimisation: lifestyle (diet, exercise, smoking cessation), metformin first-line for diabetes (also has modest antipsychotic-associated weight benefit), statin per absolute CV risk, antihypertensive per BP. Switch to a lower-risk antipsychotic (aripiprazole, lurasidone) is considered if disease is severe and switch is acceptable to the patient.
key_points: Olanzapine and clozapine highest metabolic risk
RANZCP monitoring: baseline, 3 months, 6 months, annual
Metformin first-line for new T2DM; statin per CV risk
Switch to aripiprazole or lurasidone if severe and acceptable
explanation.pearls: Metformin 500 to 2000 mg daily reduces antipsychotic-induced weight gain by 3 to 5 kg over 12 weeks even without diabetes; consider in patients with greater than 7 percent weight gain on antipsychotic.

stem: You are the consult psychiatry registrar called to ED for a 64-year-old woman brought in by her family because of 5 days of progressive immobility, mutism and refusal of food and fluid. She has a history of bipolar I disorder controlled on lithium and valproate. She was discharged 2 weeks ago after a depressive episode. On examination she stares fixedly, holds postures imposed by the examiner, and does not respond verbally. There is no fever, no rigidity, no rash and no recent medication change. Her observations are otherwise normal.

data_table.Examination: Mutism; immobility; posturing (maintains examiner-placed arm position 90 seconds); waxy flexibility; staring; no rigidity, no clonus
data_table.Investigations: Creatine kinase 280, FBC normal, electrolytes normal, lithium 0.7, valproate 480, BSL 5.8, urine bHCG negative; ECG normal sinus
data_table.Medications: Lithium 750 mg nocte; sodium valproate 1000 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg as a first-line antipsychotic to treat presumed psychotic stupor and observe for response
options.rationale: Antipsychotics may worsen catatonia and increase risk of neuroleptic malignant syndrome; lorazepam is the established first-line challenge. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Administer IV lorazepam 2 mg as a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge; observe response over 30 to 60 minutes for partial reversal
options.rationale: Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV is the standard catatonia challenge: 70 to 80 percent of catatonia responds at least partially within 10 to 60 minutes, supporting both diagnosis and treatment plan. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Discontinue lithium and valproate and observe over 48 hours for clinical improvement before any further pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Untreated catatonia carries risk of dehydration, venous thromboembolism, pressure injury and aspiration pneumonia; immediate intervention rather than withdrawal observation is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Arrange urgent electroconvulsive therapy as first-line therapy without a prior lorazepam trial or other diagnostic intervention attempt
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective in catatonia but is generally reserved for lorazepam-refractory or life-threatening cases (malignant catatonia, NMS-like presentation); lorazepam trial is first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Commence subcutaneous heparin and intravenous fluids only; defer catatonia-specific intervention until psychiatric consultant review tomorrow
options.rationale: Supportive measures (DVT prophylaxis, hydration) are important adjuncts but are not the primary intervention; lorazepam challenge should not be deferred. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a syndrome of psychomotor disturbance with mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility, negativism, echolalia and stupor. DSM-5 requires 3 or more of 12 features. Causes include mood disorders (most common in Australia), psychosis, autoimmune encephalitis, metabolic and toxic. Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV is both diagnostic challenge (response in 70 to 80 percent within 30 to 60 minutes) and therapeutic; titrate to effect, often 8 to 24 mg daily. ECT is highly effective and indicated for lorazepam non-response or malignant catatonia (autonomic instability, rigidity, hyperthermia).
key_points: Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV is diagnostic and therapeutic
70 to 80 percent partial response within 60 minutes
Avoid antipsychotics (worsen or precipitate NMS)
ECT for refractory or malignant catatonia
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia includes fever, autonomic instability and rigidity overlapping clinically with NMS; both are treated with urgent ECT plus benzodiazepines and stopping the offending drug.

subtopic_detail: Smoking cessation pharmacotherapy in psychiatric patient
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 38-year-old man with paranoid schizophrenia stable on aripiprazole 15 mg daily for 3 years. He smokes 25 cigarettes daily, smokes within 15 minutes of waking, and has tried cold turkey 4 times unsuccessfully. He is motivated to quit and asks for the best option. He has no history of depression, no recent suicidality, no cardiovascular disease, no seizure history. His current mental state is stable. He has tried 8 weeks of nicotine patches alone last year with relapse at week 4.

data_table.Examination: Stable mental state, no thought disorder; nicotine staining on fingers
data_table.Investigations: FBC, LFT, UEC normal; ECG normal sinus rhythm; PHQ-9 score 4

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Repeat nicotine patch monotherapy at higher dose (21 mg per 24 hours) for 12 weeks without combination or alternative therapy
options.rationale: Patch monotherapy was tried last year and failed at week 4; escalation to varenicline or combination NRT is more effective. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Avoid varenicline due to historical black box warnings and use bupropion 150 mg twice daily as the safer first-line agent in schizophrenia
options.rationale: The black box warning was removed in 2016 after the EAGLES trial showed no excess neuropsychiatric harm; bupropion lowers seizure threshold and is less effective than varenicline. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Commence varenicline 0.5 mg daily titrated up to 1 mg twice daily over 7 days; set quit date for 8 days; review at 4 weeks for mood and tolerance
options.rationale: Varenicline is the most effective single-agent smoking cessation pharmacotherapy and is safe in stable psychiatric disease per the EAGLES trial; standard titration over 7 days reaches 1 mg twice daily. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Refer to behavioural counselling only without pharmacotherapy given his stable psychiatric illness and previous medication trials
options.rationale: Behavioural support alone has lower quit rates than combined behavioural plus pharmacotherapy; both are recommended together. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Prescribe nortriptyline 75 mg nocte for cessation as a tricyclic with proven cessation efficacy and antidepressant effect
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is second-line for smoking cessation with cardiac and anticholinergic risks; varenicline is first-line in motivated patients without contraindication. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Smoking cessation in psychiatric patients is safe and improves both physical and mental health. First-line pharmacotherapies are varenicline, combination NRT (patch plus short-acting), or bupropion. Varenicline is the single most effective agent. The EAGLES trial established safety in stable schizophrenia, depression and other psychiatric conditions, leading to removal of the FDA black box warning in 2016. Standard varenicline titration: 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily, set quit date at day 8, continue for 12 weeks. Bupropion is avoided in seizure disorder, eating disorders and abrupt alcohol or sedative withdrawal.
key_points: Varenicline most effective single agent for cessation
EAGLES trial: safe in stable psychiatric disease
Bupropion lowers seizure threshold
Combine pharmacotherapy with behavioural support
explanation.pearls: Smoking induces CYP1A2 metabolism; quit reduces clozapine and olanzapine clearance and may require 30 to 50 percent dose reduction over 1 to 4 weeks.
